title: "Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies"

description: |
  How you generate an H-step forecast is a modeling decision in its own right.
  This article compares the recursive, direct, and joint (MIMO) strategies in
  terms of error propagation, the bias-variance trade-off, computational cost,
  and the conditions under which each one wins, with runnable code.

## 1. The question hidden inside "predict the next 24 hours"

Most introductions to time series forecasting train a model to predict a single
step ahead and stop there. But real applications almost never want one value.
An energy operator needs the next 24 hours of load, a retailer needs the next
six weeks of demand, a clinical model needs the next several days of bed
occupancy. The moment the horizon $H$ exceeds 1, a question appears that the
one-step setup never forced you to answer: *how do you produce $H$ values from
a model that, fundamentally, learns a mapping from past to future?*

This is not a hyperparameter detail. The strategy you pick changes which loss
the model actually minimizes, how forecast errors accumulate across the
horizon, how many models you have to train and store, and whether the
dependency structure between $\hat{y}_{N+1}$ and $\hat{y}_{N+5}$ is preserved or
thrown away. Three strategies dominate practice: **recursive**, **direct**, and
**joint** (the latter usually called *Multiple-Output* or *MIMO* in the
literature). They embody genuinely different assumptions, and the empirical
record on which is best is famously inconsistent — @taieb2012 found contradictory
conclusions across the prior literature, with some studies favoring recursive,
others direct, others multi-output.

Throughout, a multi-step task means predicting the next $H$ values
$[y_{N+1}, \dots, y_{N+H}]$ of a series $[y_1, \dots, y_N]$, with $H > 1$ the
forecast horizon and $d$ the number of past lags used as input (the embedding
dimension).

## 2. Recursive: one model, fed its own output

The recursive (also *iterated*) strategy is the oldest and most intuitive. You
train a single one-step model $f$:

$$
y_{t+1} = f(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + w_t,
$$ {#eq-recursive-train}

where $y_t \in \mathbb{R}$ is the value at time $t$, the input is the vector of
the $d$ most recent lags $(y_t, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, $w_t$ is
the noise term, and $f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ is the learned mapping. The
model is trained **only** on the one-step objective.

To forecast $H$ steps, you apply $\hat{f}$ repeatedly, feeding each prediction
back in as if it were an observation:

$$
\hat{y}_{N+h} =
\begin{cases}
\hat{f}(y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+1}) & h = 1 \\[4pt]
\hat{f}(\hat{y}_{N+h-1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+1}, y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+h}) & 2 \le h \le d \\[4pt]
\hat{f}(\hat{y}_{N+h-1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+h-d}) & h > d
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-recursive-forecast}

The middle case is the important one: as soon as $h \ge 2$, at least one input is
itself a prediction, not an observation. By the time $h > d$, *every* input is a
prediction. This is where the strategy's well-known weakness comes from — errors
feed forward. A small mistake at step 1 becomes part of the input at step 2,
which produces a larger mistake, and so on. The model was never trained on its
own noisy outputs, so it has no chance to learn to be robust to them; the
training and forecasting input distributions differ.

The theoretical statement behind this is that minimizing one-step error does not
minimize $H$-step error, and the recursive strategy is asymptotically biased
[@taieb_rectify]. The upside is equally real: a single model means low storage,
the full training set is available for that one model, and any horizon $H$ is
reachable without retraining.

## 3. Direct: one model per horizon

The direct strategy attacks the bias problem head-on by training a *separate*
model for each step. For each $h \in \{1, \dots, H\}$ you fit $f_h$:

$$
y_{t+h} = f_h(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + w_t,
$$ {#eq-direct-train}

so $f_h: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ maps the same $d$ observed lags directly to
the value $h$ steps ahead. Forecasts are then produced in one shot, with no
feedback:

$$
\hat{y}_{N+h} = \hat{f}_h(y_N, y_{N-1}, \dots, y_{N-d+1}), \quad h = 1, \dots, H.
$$ {#eq-direct-forecast}

Because every $\hat{f}_h$ takes only *observed* values as input, there is no
error accumulation — the failure mode of the recursive strategy is structurally
absent. Each model also optimizes exactly the objective you care about for its
own horizon, which is why the direct strategy is theoretically unbiased
[@taieb_rectify].

The cost shows up in three places. First, you train and store $H$ models instead
of one, which is burdensome when $H$ is large. Second, each model is trained
independently, so the forecasts ignore the dependency structure across the
horizon: nothing ties $\hat{y}_{N+5}$ to $\hat{y}_{N+4}$, which can produce
jagged, mutually inconsistent trajectories. Third, the effective number of
training examples shrinks for longer horizons, and being unbiased gives no
guarantee of being *consistent* in finite samples [@taieb_rectify].

## 4. Joint (MIMO): one model, a vector output

The joint strategy — Multi-Input Multi-Output, MIMO — trains a single model that
emits the entire horizon at once. Instead of a scalar output, $F$ returns a
vector:

$$
[y_{t+1}, \dots, y_{t+H}] = F(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + \mathbf{w}_t,
$$ {#eq-mimo-train}

where now $F: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^H$ maps the $d$ input lags to an
$H$-dimensional output vector in a single pass, and $\mathbf{w}_t \in
\mathbb{R}^H$ is a vector noise term. The forecast is simply

$$
[\hat{y}_{N+1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+H}] = \hat{F}(y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+1}).
$$ {#eq-mimo-forecast}

Two properties follow. There is no recursion, so no error accumulation. And
because all $H$ outputs come from one model with shared parameters, the strategy
*preserves the stochastic dependency* between forecasted values — the motivation
that introduced MIMO in the first place [@taieb2012]. A neural network with an
$H$-unit output layer, or a sequence-to-sequence decoder, is the natural modern
implementation.

The trade-off is flexibility. A single shared model imposes the same functional
form on every horizon, even though the relationship between the input window and
$h=1$ may differ from the relationship at $h=H$. The intermediate **DIRMO**
strategy was proposed precisely to interpolate here, predicting blocks of size
$s$ so that $H/s$ models each emit an $s$-vector, trading off dependency
preservation against per-horizon flexibility [@taieb2012].

One subtlety matters for the experiment below. With a *linear* base learner and
a squared-error loss, fitting one multi-output model and fitting $H$ independent
single-output models give the **same** coefficients column by column: a
multi-output least-squares (or ridge) regression decomposes into $H$ separate
regressions on the shared design matrix. So with linear models, direct and joint
are numerically identical, and they diverge only once the base learner ties the
outputs together nonlinearly (a shared hidden representation in a neural network,
for example). The code makes this concrete.

## 5. Putting the trade-offs side by side

| Property | Recursive | Direct | Joint (MIMO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Models trained | 1 | $H$ | 1 |
| Output per call | scalar | scalar ($\times H$) | vector $\mathbb{R}^H$ |
| Error accumulation | yes | no | no |
| Train/forecast input match | no (uses own preds) | yes | yes |
| Cross-horizon dependency | implicit | ignored | preserved |
| Per-horizon flexibility | none (shared $f$) | full | none (shared $F$) |
| Asymptotic bias | biased | unbiased | depends on $F$ class |
| Storage / training cost | low | high | low |

The table makes the core tension visible. Recursive and joint both use one model
(cheap), but recursive pays for it with error accumulation while joint pays with
inflexibility. Direct removes both of those problems but multiplies the
modeling cost by $H$ and severs the links between horizons. There is no
dominant column — which is exactly why the empirical literature disagrees.

## 6. A reproducible comparison

The code below runs an honest comparison on a single synthetic series. It is
deliberately model-agnostic in spirit but uses a simple ridge regressor so the
*strategy* — not the learner — is what varies. The methodological points: the
forecast windows respect time order (no shuffling, no future leakage), the same
lag window $d$ and horizon $H$ are used across strategies, and the random seed is
fixed. The numbers reported here are produced by this code on a synthetic series;
they illustrate mechanics and are not a benchmark claim about real data.

::: {#setup .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

SEED = 42
np.random.seed(SEED)

# Synthetic series: linear trend + 24-period seasonality + Gaussian noise.
N = 600
t = np.arange(N)
series = (
    0.01 * t
    + 2.0 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 24)
    + np.random.normal(0, 0.5, size=N)
)

D = 24    # embedding dimension (lags used as input)
H = 12    # forecast horizon
TEST = 100  # held-out tail used for rolling-origin evaluation
print(f"series length={N}, lags D={D}, horizon H={H}, test tail={TEST}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
series length=600, lags D=24, horizon H=12, test tail=100
```
:::
:::


::: {#windowing .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def make_supervised(s, d, h):
    """X: (n_samples, d) past lags; Y: (n_samples, h) next-h targets.
    Time order is preserved; row i never sees data after its own window."""
    X, Y = [], []
    for i in range(d, len(s) - h + 1):
        X.append(s[i - d:i])      # d past observations
        Y.append(s[i:i + h])      # h future targets
    return np.array(X), np.array(Y)

train_series = series[:-TEST]
X_tr, Y_tr = make_supervised(train_series, D, H)
print("X_tr shape:", X_tr.shape, "| Y_tr shape:", Y_tr.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
X_tr shape: (465, 24) | Y_tr shape: (465, 12)
```
:::
:::


::: {#strategies .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def fit_recursive(X, y_onestep):
    """Single model trained on the 1-step target (first column of Y)."""
    return Ridge(alpha=1.0, random_state=SEED).fit(X, y_onestep)

def forecast_recursive(model, last_window, h, d):
    win = list(last_window)
    preds = []
    for _ in range(h):
        yhat = model.predict(np.array(win[-d:]).reshape(1, -1))[0]
        preds.append(yhat)
        win.append(yhat)          # feed the prediction back in
    return np.array(preds)

def fit_direct(X, Y):
    """H independent single-output models, one per horizon."""
    return [Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, Y[:, h]) for h in range(Y.shape[1])]

def forecast_direct(models, last_window):
    x = last_window.reshape(1, -1)
    return np.array([m.predict(x)[0] for m in models])

def fit_joint(X, Y):
    """Single multi-output model: Ridge accepts vector targets directly."""
    return Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, Y)

def forecast_joint(model, last_window):
    return model.predict(last_window.reshape(1, -1))[0]

rec   = fit_recursive(X_tr, Y_tr[:, 0])
dir_  = fit_direct(X_tr, Y_tr)
joint = fit_joint(X_tr, Y_tr)
print("fitted: recursive (1 model), direct (%d models), joint (1 model)" % len(dir_))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
fitted: recursive (1 model), direct (12 models), joint (1 model)
```
:::
:::


::: {#evaluate .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Rolling-origin evaluation across the held-out tail: slide the origin,
# forecast H steps from each, compare against the realized future.
errs = {"recursive": [], "direct": [], "joint": []}
sq_by_h = {k: np.zeros(H) for k in errs}
n_windows = 0

start = len(series) - TEST
for origin in range(start, len(series) - H + 1):
    win   = series[origin - D:origin]
    truth = series[origin:origin + H]
    preds = {
        "recursive": forecast_recursive(rec, win, H, D),
        "direct":    forecast_direct(dir_, win),
        "joint":     forecast_joint(joint, win),
    }
    for k, p in preds.items():
        errs[k].append(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(truth, p)))
        sq_by_h[k] += (p - truth) ** 2
    n_windows += 1

print(f"rolling windows evaluated: {n_windows}\n")
print("mean RMSE over the full 12-step horizon")
for k in errs:
    print(f"  {k:10s} {np.mean(errs[k]):.4f}")

print("\nRMSE at short horizon (h=1..3) vs long horizon (h=10..12)")
for k in sq_by_h:
    rmse_h = np.sqrt(sq_by_h[k] / n_windows)
    print(f"  {k:10s} short={rmse_h[:3].mean():.4f}  long={rmse_h[-3:].mean():.4f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
rolling windows evaluated: 89

mean RMSE over the full 12-step horizon
  recursive  0.4733
  direct     0.4868
  joint      0.4868

RMSE at short horizon (h=1..3) vs long horizon (h=10..12)
  recursive  short=0.4896  long=0.4764
  direct     short=0.4957  long=0.4933
  joint      short=0.4957  long=0.4933
```
:::
:::


The output shows three things worth reading carefully. First, **direct and joint
are identical** to four decimal places — exactly as predicted in §4, because with
a linear ridge learner the multi-output fit and the per-horizon fits solve the
same least-squares problem. Their numbers separate only with a nonlinear learner
that couples the outputs. Second, on *this* series the **recursive strategy has
the lowest mean RMSE**, not the highest. The clean "recursive is biased, so it
loses" story does not hold here: the data-generating process is close to the
one-step model's functional form, the noise is modest, and the dynamics are
stable, so error accumulation stays small while the recursive model benefits from
using the full training set on a single well-specified target. Third, the
short-vs-long split shows the gap does not blow up with horizon on this series —
again a consequence of stable, low-noise dynamics rather than a general law.

## 7. How to read the results, and what the literature actually says

The clean theoretical story — recursive is biased but low-variance, direct is
unbiased but higher-variance, joint preserves dependency — is a useful guide but
an unreliable predictor, and the run above is a small live demonstration of that.
Across published comparisons the rankings flip with the dataset, the base
learner, and the horizon. @taieb2012, running 111 series from the NN5
competition, found multiple-output (joint-family) strategies to be the best
performers overall, and also found that deseasonalizing the series before
forecasting improved accuracy uniformly across strategies — a reminder that
preprocessing can matter more than the strategy choice. A recent analysis
revisits the bias-variance framing itself and reports that recursive strategies
can match or beat direct ones in practice despite the latter looking
theoretically preferable [@green2025epistemic], attributing the gap to factors
the textbook decomposition omits. The result above, where recursive wins on a
smooth synthetic series, is consistent with that more cautious reading.

The practical takeaway: do not select a strategy from first principles alone.
Hold out a realistic validation horizon, evaluate per-step error with
rolling-origin resampling as done here, and let the data on *your* series decide.
The **rectify** strategy of @taieb_rectify is worth knowing as a principled
middle ground — it starts from biased recursive forecasts and fits a direct
correction on top, and was reported to be at least as good as the better of
recursive and direct.

## 8. Limitations

This comparison is about *strategy*, holding the learner fixed; with a different
base model (gradient boosting, an LSTM, a Transformer) the relative ranking can
change, and some architectures blur the categories — a sequence-to-sequence
model is joint by construction, while an autoregressive Transformer decoder is
recursive at inference. The linear ridge learner is precisely why direct and
joint coincide above; that coincidence is an artifact of linearity, not a general
fact. The synthetic series here is smooth, low-noise, and stationary in its
seasonal structure, which flatters the recursive strategy; a noisier or more
nonlinear process can reverse the ranking, so a single dataset cannot settle the
question. Finally, none of these strategies addresses forecast *uncertainty*
directly; quantifying predictive intervals over a horizon is a separate problem
that interacts with the strategy choice in ways not covered here.

## 9. Conclusion

The choice among recursive, direct, and joint is a choice about which problem you
are willing to have. Recursive buys cheapness and any-horizon flexibility at the
price of compounding error — a price that, as the experiment shows, can be near
zero on stable low-noise data. Direct buys unbiasedness and per-horizon tailoring
at the price of $H$ models and broken cross-horizon structure. Joint buys a
single cheap model that respects the dependency across the horizon, at the price
of imposing one functional form on every step — and collapses onto direct exactly
when the learner is linear. Theory ranks them only loosely; the empirical
literature is openly contradictory; and the strategy that wins on your data is
something you should measure, per horizon, rather than assume. If you want one
default to start from, a multi-output model with proper deseasonalization is well
supported by the NN5-scale evidence — but treat it as a baseline to beat, not a
settled answer.
